section.title: The City of Our God
section.sectionRationale: Closing this Korahite triad of Zion psalms, Psalm 48 praises Jerusalem as the city of the great King, language Jesus Himself uses in Matthew 5:35, and which finds its final fulfillment in the heavenly Zion, Hebrews 12:22, and the new Jerusalem, Revelation 21.
section.thesis: Zion's God-given security, dramatically proven when enemy kings flee in terror before her, becomes cause to walk her ramparts and tell the next generation, pointing forward to the heavenly Jerusalem, the city of the living God that all who are in Christ have already come to.
section.frameNote: Four movements: praise for the great city and its holy mountain, verses 1-3; the kings who assembled and fled in terror, verses 4-8; meditation on God's loving devotion within the temple, verses 9-11; and the call to walk Zion's ramparts and tell the next generation, verses 12-14.

units.label: Great is the LORD, and greatly to be praised
units.note: praise for the great city and its holy mountain

verses.text: Great is the LORD, and greatly to be praised in the city of our God, His holy mountain.
Beautiful in loftiness, the joy of all the earth, like the peaks of {{tsapon|Zaphon}} is Mount Zion, the city of the great King.
God is in her citadels; He has shown Himself to be a fortress.

units.label: They saw and were astounded
units.note: the kings who assembled and fled in terror

verses.text: For behold, the kings assembled; they all advanced together.
They saw and were astounded; they fled in terror.
Trembling seized them there, anguish like a woman in labor.
With a wind from the east You wrecked the ships of Tarshish.
As we have heard, so we have seen in the city of the LORD of Hosts, in the city of our God: God will establish her forever. Selah

units.label: Within Your temple, O God, we contemplate Your loving devotion
units.note: meditation on God's loving devotion within the temple

verses.text: Within Your temple, O God, we contemplate Your {{chesed|loving devotion}}.

Your name, O God, like Your praise, reaches to the ends of the earth; 
Your right hand is full of righteousness.

Mount Zion is glad, the daughters of Judah rejoice, 
on account of Your judgments.

units.label: This God is our God forever and ever
units.note: the call to walk Zion's ramparts and tell the next generation

verses.text: March around Zion, encircle her, count her towers,
consider her ramparts, tour her citadels, that you may tell the next generation.
For this God is our God forever and ever; He will be our guide even till death.

tsapon.gloss: Zaphon, the mythical mountain of the gods in Canaanite religion, believed to be the dwelling place of Baal. The psalmist provocatively claims this exalted image for Mount Zion instead, declaring the true God's mountain surpasses every rival claim.

chesed.gloss: Steadfast, covenant loving devotion. Within the temple itself, the worshiping community's central meditation is not on the building's grandeur but on this quality of God's loyal, covenant love.

basic.context: This Zion psalm celebrates Jerusalem, God's holy mountain, as beautiful and secure, dramatically proven when a coalition of enemy kings saw the city, were struck with terror, and fled without a battle. Worshipers are called to walk its ramparts, count its towers, and tell the next generation of God's faithfulness.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm's confidence rests not in Jerusalem's natural defenses but entirely in God's presence within her, so that the city's security becomes a living testimony, walked and counted and retold, of God's own faithfulness across generations.
basic.originalLanguages: Mount Zion is compared to the peaks of tsapon, Zaphon, the mythical mountain of the gods in Canaanite religion, provocatively claiming that grandeur for the true God's own mountain instead. Within the temple, worshipers meditate on God's chesed, His steadfast covenant loving devotion.
basic.typology: Mount Zion, the city of the great King, is the very title Jesus applies to God's throne in Matthew 5:35, while the psalm's confidence in an unshakeable, eternal city anticipates the heavenly Zion of Hebrews 12:22 and the new Jerusalem of Revelation 21, where God dwells with His people forever.
basic.christ: Jesus directly echoes this psalm's language when He calls Jerusalem 'the city of the great King,' Matthew 5:35, speaking with the very authority of Zion's true King, while the psalm's vision of an eternally established city finds its complete fulfillment in the heavenly Jerusalem believers have already come to in Christ, Hebrews 12:22-24.
basic.god: God is shown as the true defender of His city, present within her citadels, whose reputation and righteousness reach to the ends of the earth, and whose loving devotion is the very substance worshipers contemplate within His temple.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 5:34-35 has Jesus using this psalm's own title for Jerusalem, the city of the great King. Hebrews 12:22-24 declares that believers have already come to Mount Zion, the heavenly Jerusalem. Revelation 21:2-3 pictures the new Jerusalem where God dwells with His people forever.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's celebration of Zion's God-given, unshakeable security, dramatically proven before fleeing kings, points forward to the heavenly Jerusalem, the city of the great King that Jesus Himself names, and that all who are in Christ have already come to and will dwell in forever.

context.literary: A song of Zion closing the Korahite triad of Psalms 46-48, structured around praise for the city, verses 1-3, its dramatic deliverance from besieging kings, verses 4-8, meditation on God's loving devotion within the temple, verses 9-11, and a closing call to walk and retell Zion's security, verses 12-14.
context.historical: Most plausibly recalls a specific historical deliverance of Jerusalem, such as the miraculous defeat of Sennacherib's Assyrian siege in Hezekiah's reign, 2 Kings 19, when a coalition of enemy forces withdrew from the city without a battle, exactly as this psalm describes.
context.canonical: Closes a trilogy of Zion psalms, 46 through 48, celebrating God's presence, kingship, and unshakeable city, themes the New Testament takes up directly in describing both Christ's authority over Jerusalem, Matthew 5:35, and the believer's citizenship in the heavenly Zion, Hebrews 12:22.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To ground the worshiping community's confidence in God's own presence and faithfulness, proven in a dramatic deliverance, and to call every generation to walk, examine, and retell that faithfulness so it is never forgotten.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a specific historical deliverance of Jerusalem from a coalition of enemy kings; it prescribes as an abiding pattern remembering and retelling God's faithfulness to the next generation, and resting security in God's presence rather than in human defenses.

debates.question: Is the defeat of the assembled kings in verses 4-7 a specific historical deliverance or a generalized liturgical picture of God's protection of Zion?

views.label: The psalm recalls a specific historical deliverance, most plausibly the failed Assyrian siege under Sennacherib in Hezekiah's reign, 2 Kings 19
views.case: The precise details, kings assembling and advancing together, then fleeing in terror without a recorded battle, closely match the sudden, inexplicable withdrawal of Assyria's army described in 2 Kings 19:35-36.

views.label: The psalm is deliberately unspecific, a generalized liturgical celebration of God's defense of Zion usable for any threat, not tied to one particular battle
views.case: The psalm names no specific enemy or king, using generic language, 'the kings,' that would allow the community to sing it in response to any threat across generations, not only one historical crisis.

debates.bearing: Either way, the psalm functions the same pastorally, inviting God's people in any age of threat to walk Zion's ramparts and retell what God has done, whether recalling one great deliverance or God's pattern of faithful defense across many.

study.originalLanguages: Mount Zion is likened to the peaks of tsapon, Zaphon, the mythical mountain of the gods in Canaanite religion, a bold claim that the true God's mountain surpasses every pagan rival. Within the temple, the community's meditation centers on chesed, God's steadfast, covenant loving devotion.

typology.type: Mount Zion, the city of the great King, verse 2
typology.fulfillment: Jesus applies this very title to God's throne, Matthew 5:34-35, and the writer of Hebrews shows believers have already come to the heavenly Zion, Hebrews 12:22.

typology.type: God establishes the city forever, verse 8, and is our guide even to death, verse 14
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in the eternal city of Revelation 21:2-3, the new Jerusalem where God dwells with His people forever.

study.christ: Jesus directly echoes this psalm's own language when He warns against swearing by Jerusalem, 'for it is the city of the great King,' Matthew 5:35, speaking with the authority of Zion's true King Himself. The psalm's confidence in God's unshakeable, eternally established city meets its final fulfillment in the heavenly Jerusalem, which the writer of Hebrews declares believers have already come to, Hebrews 12:22-24, and which appears in its consummate form in Revelation 21:2-3, where Christ the Lamb is Himself its lamp.
study.god: God is the true defender of His city, present within her citadels so completely that enemy kings flee at the mere sight of her, His name and righteousness reaching to the ends of the earth, and His steadfast loving devotion the very substance of worship within His temple.

crossReferences.note: Jesus uses this psalm's own title for Jerusalem, the city of the great King, speaking with Zion's true King's own authority.
Declares that believers in Christ have already come to Mount Zion, the heavenly Jerusalem.
Pictures the new Jerusalem descending, where God dwells with His people forever, the final fulfillment of this psalm's eternal city.

study.oneStory: This psalm's celebration of Zion's God-given, unshakeable security, dramatically proven when kings fled in terror at the mere sight of her, points forward to the heavenly Jerusalem, the city of the great King that Jesus Himself names and that all who are in Christ have already come to and will dwell in forever.

sermon.title: Psalm 48
sermon.why: Carson expounds Psalm 48's celebration of Jerusalem as an image of God's kingship and protection, tracing the psalm's trajectory from the historical city to the New Jerusalem of Christian hope, directly serving this study's movement from the earthly Zion to its heavenly fulfillment.

quiz.q: How does verse 2 describe Mount Zion?

options.text: Beautiful in loftiness, the joy of all the earth, the city of the great King.
options.note: This description claims for Zion the very grandeur pagan religion attributed to the mythical mountain Zaphon.

options.text: A small, insignificant hill of no particular beauty.
options.note: The verse explicitly praises Zion's beauty and loftiness, not insignificance.

options.text: A location soon to be permanently abandoned.
options.note: The psalm celebrates God's eternal establishment of this city, not its abandonment.

options.text: A city known mainly for its marketplace.
options.note: The verse's focus is on the city's beauty and its identity as the city of the great King, not commerce.

quiz.q: What happened to the assembled kings, according to verses 4-6?

options.text: They saw the city, were astounded, and fled in terror.
options.note: The kings withdrew without any recorded battle, a dramatic proof of God's defense of the city.

options.text: They successfully conquered the city.
options.note: The text describes their terrified flight, not a successful conquest.

options.text: They negotiated a peace treaty with the city's defenders.
options.note: No negotiation is described; the kings simply fled in terror at what they saw.

options.text: They laid siege for many years before withdrawing.
options.note: The text describes a sudden, dramatic flight upon seeing the city, not a prolonged siege.

quiz.q: What are worshipers instructed to do in verses 12-13?

options.text: March around Zion, count her towers, consider her ramparts, and tell the next generation.
options.note: This active remembrance ensures God's faithfulness is retold and not forgotten by future generations.

options.text: Avoid the city entirely out of fear.
options.note: The instruction is to actively approach, walk around, and examine the city, not avoid it.

options.text: Tear down the city's towers as unnecessary.
options.note: The towers are to be counted and considered as testimony to God's protection, not destroyed.

options.text: Keep the knowledge of God's deliverance secret from others.
options.note: The explicit instruction is to tell the next generation, the opposite of secrecy.

quiz.q: What does verse 14 declare about God?

options.text: He is our God forever and ever, and will be our guide even to death.
options.note: This closing declaration grounds all the psalm's confidence in God's abiding, eternal relationship with His people.

options.text: He will abandon His people once the present crisis ends.
options.note: The verse declares the opposite, an everlasting relationship and guidance, not abandonment.

options.text: He is only concerned with the physical city's defenses.
options.note: The verse's scope is a personal, eternal relationship, our guide even to death, not merely physical defenses.

options.text: He will guide us only until we reach old age.
options.note: The text specifically says His guidance continues even to death, not stopping earlier.

quiz.q: Where does Jesus quote or echo this psalm's language about Jerusalem?

options.text: Matthew 5:35, calling Jerusalem the city of the great King.
options.note: Jesus applies this exact psalm's title for Jerusalem to Himself as the true King with authority over the city.

options.text: The parable of the prodigal son.
options.note: That parable does not reference Jerusalem or this psalm's language about the city of the great King.

options.text: The Sermon on the Mount's teaching on fasting.
options.note: The teaching on fasting is unrelated to this psalm's language about Jerusalem as the city of the great King.

options.text: The cleansing of the temple.
options.note: While that event occurs in Jerusalem, Jesus's direct quotation of this psalm's title occurs in the Sermon on the Mount, Matthew 5:35.

questions: This psalm praises Zion's beauty as the city of the great King even before describing any deliverance. How often do you praise God simply for who He is, before asking Him for anything?

The enemy kings fled in terror simply from seeing the city, without any battle being fought. Where in your own life have you seen God provide deliverance in ways you did not have to fight for yourself?

Worshipers are told to walk Zion's ramparts and count her towers so they can tell the next generation, verses 12-13. What specific evidence of God's faithfulness in your own life could you deliberately pass on to someone younger this week?

Jesus calls Jerusalem 'the city of the great King,' echoing this very psalm, Matthew 5:35. How does recognizing Christ's own authority over this psalm's language change how you read its confidence in God's protection?
The psalm closes with the declaration, 'this God is our God forever, He will be our guide even to death.' What would change in how you face uncertainty if you held onto this same confidence today?

translationNotes.note: Zaphon renders the Hebrew tsapon, the mythical mountain of the gods in Canaanite religion, here provocatively claimed for the true God's own mountain, Zion, rather than any pagan rival.
The ships of Tarshish were large seagoing trade vessels, a proverbial symbol of human wealth and power broken here before God's power, compare Isaiah 2:16.
Our guide even till death renders a phrase some ancient traditions read as our guide forever, but the sense in either case is God's abiding, unending guidance of His people.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 48:1-14. No em or en dashes present. Sermon link verified this session via WebSearch confirming D. A. Carson's message titled Psalm 48 at thegospelcoalition.org, whose content directly expounds Jerusalem, God's kingship, and the New Jerusalem trajectory of this passage; direct page fetch was blocked by a session rate limit, but the indexed title, author, and content summary confirm the match.
